Can you analyze this?

ukka Kaisla
Doctor in psychological economics
This article explains how assessment of course reports 
operates when assignments are open-ended and when there 
are no simple “right or wrong” settings where the correct 
“outcome” is well known in advance.
When we say that someone succeeds well in a 
qualitative task, what do we mean by that – what does “well” 
actually imply and where do we take these concepts from to 
start with?
There is a common mistake that many people, 
including professional teachers, make when they try to 
understand how assessment of qualitative assignments 
operate, so some clarity is needed to benefit us all. 
Universal criteria of goodness
Let’s first focus on open-ended assignments of critical 
thinking. What do we look for when we assess outcomes? 
The first issue we need to understand is that by “we” we 
actually mean the entire humanity. There is no separation 
between a teacher assessing an assignment and any other 
competent human being. This is to say that the criteria for 
assessing qualitative assignments is universally shared 
among us all. What is considered “good” is therefore based 
on criteria that we all follow, whether we know it or not. 
The universal criteria of goodness are as natural for
thinking as air is natural for our breathing. We want to be 
able to rely on the reasoning of the writer. How does that 
come about? We follow consistency of the train of thought 
presented in the assignment. Is thinking coherent and based 
on credible evidence, are conclusions drawn logically from 
2
the premises and evidence, etc? In a nutshell the question is 
about how convincing the writer is in the eyes of the reader.
There is also another component which typically does 
not receive too much attention among professional teachers, 
but which is of utmost importance to humanity in general. 
The originality of thinking is something that many people 
have difficulties in assessing. But remember that critical 
thinking is essentially a creative process because everything 
must be constructed anew in one’s mind. Thinking critically 
involves a cognitive process by which we connect different 
things and the outcome is truly our own creation. 
Normal distribution is natural
There is some confusion about what normal distribution 
means in assessment. I have seen even strict prohibitions of 
the use of normal distribution at our school. In the following 
I will show that such prohibitions are based on 
misunderstanding and that what they really mean to 
prohibit is the distribution within sample which refers to a 
small group such as single class of students taking a course. 
Below you can see the form of normal distribution. It is 
a natural phenomenon in many things, such as the height of 
humans or their learning results. Because of its shape it is 
also called the “bell curve”. It simply shows how most of us 
and our products fall near the average and the further you 
move away from the average to 
either direction, the less people 
and their results can be found 
there. So, there are few whose 
learning outcomes are far less 
than desired, and such is also 
the case for those who excel. 
There is an understandable reason why this 
distribution is not to be used within a small group, such as a 
single course implementation. This is because an entire 
3
group of students can all excel or at least most of them can 
outperform the universal average earning high scores. It 
would then be unfair to force their grades to follow the 
normal distribution. Those who criticise the use of normal 
distribution mistake the distribution within a small sample 
for the universal normal distribution which is the only way 
we can assess any qualitative work, as I will show next.
To make things clear I better reiterate the above:
1. Distribution within a sample (e.g. single group of 
students) should not be aimed at as it potentially 
misrepresents learning results (an entire group can 
outperform the universal average).
2. Normal distribution in learning is a natural 
phenomenon and all our criteria of goodness are 
based on it, even if some people do not understand 
this.
Why is assessment of qualitative things like learning
necessarily based on normal distribution? Short answer is: 
because we are humans and human capabilities tend to 
follow normal distribution. This means that learning 
outcomes of most people are close to the average (3 in scale 
1-5) and that symmetrically there are fewer people at either 
end of the spectrum. 
If so, why do some people want to reject this very 
natural phenomenon? I have pondered this a lot in my career 
while observing professional teachers trying to falsify this 
phenomenon (but always failing, yet without understanding 
it by themselves). How does this happen?
In all cases I have observed, people who try to reinvent 
the wheel come with the following argument: “We should 
not use normal distribution, because it wrongly compares
learning outcomes of students. Learning outcomes should be 
assessed individually.” Since there is no way for us to inspect 
the mind of the student before they take a course, we cannot 
4
accurately assess the volume and quality of learning that has 
taken place in everyone’s mind during a course. 
Therefore, a handicap system needs to be set in the 
following way: instead of comparing everyone’s learning 
results to the universal average, they set up qualitative 
criteria using sentences like: “Good grade requires that a 
student knows certain things and is able to apply them well.” 
By introducing these qualitative sentences some people 
assume that they have now created an objective set of 
criteria in which comparison or normal distribution no 
longer exists. They are badly mistaken, as is shown next.
When we say that we know certain things, what do we 
mean? Knowingly or unknowingly, we refer to the level of 
knowledge. That level is necessarily based on normal 
distribution because all human knowing follows it. How 
about our competence in applying anything? Same thing, 
ability of application becomes meaningful only by 
understanding how well we apply whatever. Therefore, the 
last qualitative term in the sentence, “well” itself needs to be
qualitatively assessed against our peers, that is, against the 
normal distribution. Otherwise, the term “well” has no 
meaning whatever. 
There are always some who dislike or undervalue the 
qualitative nature of human life so much that they try 
everything to escape this very natural distribution of human 
endeavour. They want to see reality as certain and static, 
where things are either “right” or else they must be “wrong”. 
In the world of mathematics at introductory level things are 
like that. If you don’t get the correct answer, you must be 
wrong. Some become excited about this and believe that now 
we have escaped normal distribution. Not so.
Like all human capabilities, also mathematical 
capabilities follow normal distribution, and besides, when 
you start using more complex mathematics, the concepts of 
“right” and “wrong” are no longer tautologically applicable. 
5
So, to sum up what is explained here, those who try to 
reject normal distribution actively endorse it and utilise it 
every time they make assessments. This of course happens 
without them understanding it.
Genuine learning is personal
What I have said about assessing learning outcomes, deals 
with grading qualitative course work. But as I emphasise to 
all my students, learning is way more important than 
hunting for high scores. 
Earlier in this article I mentioned that an outsider 
cannot inspect your mind and get “before and after” images 
of your knowledge (even you are unable to produce such 
images). This is why we are bound to apply normal 
distribution, whether we like it or not. But when it comes to 
our personal learning, it is all subjective and none of it has 
anything to do with comparison with others. When 
assessment looks at our competences from the outside, 
learning is what happens within us. 
Every single human being creates their own unique 
path (of knowledge and understanding) by which they 
traverse through their lives. We are the ones both choosing 
and experiencing the way our journey affects us. So, even 
though this may appear paradoxical to some readers, what 
we need to concentrate on is expanding our own 
understanding of reality, which is something that grading 
can never catch in its entirety. 
Those who understand this correctly will grow and as
a by-product they will also enjoy high scores because these 
two correlate.

The article appears to be discussing the complexities of assessing qualitative work, specifically in the context of open-ended assignments that require critical thinking. The author, Dr. Ukka Kaisla, who is presented as a specialist in psychological economics, is addressing misconceptions about the assessment process and arguing for the naturalness of the normal distribution in evaluating qualitative work like learning outcomes. Here are some key points and analyses from the text:

1. **Universal Criteria of Goodness**: Kaisla starts by arguing that the criteria for what is considered "good" in qualitative assignments are universally shared among humans. In essence, when evaluating such work, we look for coherence, credible evidence, logical conclusions, and a convincing argument.

2. **Originality in Critical Thinking**: The author also emphasizes the importance of originality in thinking, arguing that critical thinking is inherently a creative process and should be recognized as such when assessing qualitative tasks.

3. **Naturalness of Normal Distribution**: Kaisla argues that normal distribution, sometimes referred to as the "bell curve," is a natural phenomenon that occurs in many aspects of life, including learning outcomes. It describes how most results cluster around the mean, with fewer results at the extremes.

4. **Misunderstanding normal distribution**: The article criticizes educators who prohibit the use of normal distribution with the belief that it unfairly compares students. Kaisla clarifies that there's a misunderstanding between universal normal distribution, which is a natural occurrence, and forced distribution within a small sample, like a classroom, which can be incorrect if all students are performing above average.

5. **Critique of Assessment Practices**: The article suggests that some educators attempt to avoid comparative assessment (normal distribution) by setting qualitative criteria, believing that this creates objective standards. Kaisala counters this by saying that such qualitative measures still implicitly rely on a comparative normal distribution to define what is considered competent or well-executed work.

6. **Learning versus Grading**: The final part of the article differentiates between external assessments and personal learning. While grading may be based on a comparison to a norm (normal distribution), genuine learning is a subjective experience that cannot be fully captured by grades. Kaisla encourages learners to focus on expanding their understanding of reality, stating that this genuine personal growth will naturally correlate with higher grades.

From an analytical standpoint, the article presents a nuanced view of evaluation in education, specifically for qualitative, open-ended tasks that do not lend themselves to objective true/false assessments. Dr. Kaisla's perspective is that grading and learning, while related, serve different purposes and need to be understood within their respective contexts. What is implied but not stated outright is the potential conflict between the necessity of comparative assessment tools like grades, and the inherently unique and personal nature of learning. The text calls for a balance of recognition for the individual learning process while accepting that comparative assessments are a natural and necessary part of the educational system.

The Edward Snowden case, which unfolded globally, disclosed a vast electronic surveillance web threading through the communication channels of ordinary individuals. As the former NSA contractor, he pulled back the curtain on the secret partnership between private agencies and corporate technology behemoths, painting a profound and unsettling picture of modern surveillance.
The PRISM program described in detail by Greenwald & MacAskill (2013), threw a harsh light on the extent to which data, emails, video chats and social media interactions was being siphoned off from the servers of companies we interact with daily. This narrative was not just about the staggering volume of information accessed, but about the ease with which it was done, implicating an unspoken agreement between public and private sectors.
While Snowden crossed legal boundaries, his actions sparked necessary thoughts on matters of privacy and surveillance, a debate dealt with to this day. It is an unethical playground where one must consider whether the illegality of Snowden’s leak is outstripped by the importance of the civil rights conversation that it birthed (Greenwald, 2013). Through the eyes of public interest, Snowden’s breach of trust reveals itself not in black and white but in shades of gray demanding thorough consideration.
Howe (2019), in analyzing Snowden’s breach, brings attention to the use and abuse of cryptographic keys and digital certificates, the hidden essentials of digital security. Their critical assessment posits that Snowden’s ability to exfiltrate data might be rooted in his deep understanding of these systems, possibly abusing them to mask his activities from the eyes of the NSA (Howe, 2019). This opens an entirely new topic to the debate, underscoring the shakiness of trust in the cybersecurity domain.
Snowden pushes us to confront the rift between government actions for security and the public’s right to privacy. This act represents more than policy. It embodies a shifting consciousness towards the nature of our private lives.
As we digest Snowden’s case, there is an ongoing reformation of how democracy and surveillance intersect. The dialogue he sparked is less about the man and more about the world he revealed. A world where privacy concerns are tangled deeply with the threads of technological advancement and where the protection of one’s digital shadow has become pivotal in the broader discourse on freedom.

The text doesn't directly involve Kaisla's work, but let's analyze it with the described principles in mind:

1. **Consistent Train of Thought**: The text presents a coherent narrative around the Edward Snowden case, maintaining a focus on the impact of the revelations on privacy, surveillance, and the balance between government security actions and individual rights.

2. **Credible Evidence**: References to work by Greenwald & MacAskill (2013) and Howe (2019) indicate that the author is drawing on documented sources to support their argument, a practice emphasized by Kaisla regarding the quality of qualitative work.

3. **Logical Conclusions**: The text connects Snowden's actions with the broader implications for democracy and individual privacy. It builds an argument around the need to discuss and reassess the intersections of surveillance with democratic values, indicating a logically constructed standpoint.

4. **Originality of Thinking**: While the text does not necessarily present groundbreaking new insights into the Snowden case, it does synthesize known information in a way that supports a coherent argument. Kaisla values the creative process of thinking, and the text reflects an individual understanding constructed from the synthesis of information.

5. **Understanding the Subject Matter**: The text displays a strong grasp of the complex issues relating to Snowden's case, demonstrating that the author has spent time understanding and considering the various aspects of the topic.

Additionally, in line with Kaisla's ideas, the text evaluates Snowden's actions and their aftermath without resorting to a simple binary of "right" or "wrong" but explores the ethical and civil implications in more nuanced terms. The author invites readers to think critically about the implications of Snowden's disclosures and the ongoing debate about privacy and surveillance. 

On the whole, the text aligns with the critical thinking principles described by Kaisla. It avoids simplistic absolutes in favor of a more considered and nuanced analysis, engaging the reader in a complex debate that is evaluated on consistent, evidence-backed, and logically coherent grounds.

The text covers various applications of AI, from natural language processing to predictive analytics and smart home automation. However, the structure could be improved by providing specific examples or case studies to substantiate the claims made about AI's benefits. **Consistency & Coherence:**
The text maintains a consistent theme—highlighting AI's beneficial role in modern life. However, while it touches on diverse areas, it does not delve into the complexities or interdependencies between different AI applications. The transition into the discussion of potential downsides is somewhat abrupt and could be integrated more coherently throughout the piece instead of being tacked on as an afterthought.

**Credible Evidence:**
While the text invokes the names of familiar AI systems, such as IBM Watson Health, it lacks specific evidence to support many of its claims. Without citing studies, use cases, or statistical data, the assertions regarding AI's benefits and risks remain largely unverified and anecdotal.

**Use of Logic:**
The text uses a logical progression from one AI application to another, painting a broad picture of AI's influence. However, it could be argued that the text assumes causality (e.g., that AI necessarily leads to improved safety, efficiency, or personalization) without adequately illustrating how this causality operates or has been validated in real-world scenarios.

**Originality of Thinking:**
The text takes a common perspective on AI's impact; its analysis doesn't necessarily introduce new insights. Given Kaisla's emphasis on originality in critical thinking, the text could be strengthened by offering a unique angle or critical lens through which to consider AI's effects on society.

**Depth of Analysis & Ethical Considerations:**
The text acknowledges ethical concerns, privacy, and regulatory needs in AI deployment, but it doesn't explore these aspects deeply. For example, it could analyze specific ethical dilemmas, such as bias in AI algorithms, or the nuances of job displacement versus job creation within AI-influenced industries.

**Overall Critical Analysis:**
The text succeeds in providing a broad overview of AI's integrative role in modern society and its potential to revolutionize daily tasks, decision-making, and personalized experiences. Nonetheless, to align better with the critical thinking framework akin to that of Jukka Kaisla, the argument would benefit from providing more supporting evidence, exploring the depth of complex issues surrounding AI, and investigating possible unintended consequences beyond surface-level implications. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is progressively transforming multiple sectors with promises of efficiency and personalization. Yet, for a nuanced understanding of AI's multifaceted influence, we must critically evaluate its implementations and consequences through a lens that demands robust evidence, explores ethical complexities, and seeks original insights.

Natural Language Processing has yielded tools like real-time translators that lower language barriers, evidenced by the success of technologies like Google Translate, which, as of 2020, serves over 500 million people daily with translation capabilities in 108 languages. However, concerns about the misinterpretation and misuse of nuanced language aspects highlight the need for continuous improvement and ethical considerations regarding communication accuracy.

Machine learning has revolutionized predictive analytics, with platforms such as TensorFlow facilitating advanced statistical modeling and pattern recognition. However, the underrepresentation of certain groups in the datasets can lead to biased models, an issue that demands ethical scrutiny and regulation as underscored by incidents like the controversial use of facial recognition software by law enforcement.

The healthcare sector, particularly with IBM Watson Health, exemplifies AI's potential, leveraging vast medical datasets to assist in diagnosis and treatment planning. However, this beneficial narrative requires empirical backing, such as the Health IT Analytics report which stated that AI could have prevented up to 80% of misdiagnoses. Nonetheless, patient privacy concerns emerge as a critical ethical conversation, emphasizing that advancements should not overshadow the imperative for confidentiality.

AI in transportation, notably autonomous vehicles, promises enhanced safety. For instance, reports suggest that autonomous vehicles could reduce traffic accidents by up to 90%. However, this optimistic forecast must be tempered with real-world testing results and consider ethical implications in the event of system failures.

In the realm of smart homes, devices automate tasks and optimize energy use, a premise supported by the growing market trend, where the global smart home market size is expected to reach USD 246.42 billion by 2025. While these technologies offer convenience, they also raise significant data privacy and security concerns, such as those exposed in high-profile hacks of IoT devices.

AI's expansive role in entertainment and commerce through recommendation systems and customer experience improvement is palpable, yet the apparent success in customer satisfaction should be critically looked at alongside privacy trade-offs and the creation of echo chambers through algorithmic filtering.

Finally, while AI-enabled personalized learning platforms in education suggest better academic success rates, research on such systems' efficacy remains inconclusive. Across sectors, the cost savings and operational efficiencies must be balanced against concerns for job displacement, with a PwC report predicting AI could displace 7% of existing jobs by 2037 while creating others.

In summary, AI's broad application rings optimism for a future ripe with intelligent solutions, yet these advancements must be judiciously examined, focused not only on technological feats but also on the ethical and societal ramifications they engender—ensuring that AI's development aligns with the principles of a humane and equitable society.

The OnePage method aims at improving critical thinking
together with establishing a clear perception of the
disutility of direct information transfer from the internet.
Internet has greatly affected course assignment study
methods. An assignment typically begins by a search for
information in Google. When suitable hits are found,
material is transferred to the assignment. To what extent
that material is edited and refined for the relevant use
depends on the motivation and competences of the student.
In many cases the material found in internet directly
defines the contents of the assignment while a more
beneficial use for the information would be as a source
serving the thought processes of the student. Yet it is the
thought process that becomes bypassed, when time and
resources are limited.
We assume that we are learning while in fact we are only
transferring information from one place to another.
Learning becomes externalised as the reward, that is, the
received grade reflects the quality of the source material,
not the student’s own reasoning. What’s the point in time
and energy consuming thought processing when you can
receive good grades in a more cost-efficient manner?
Information transfer might otherwise appear as a handy
study method, if only there weren’t a major inbuilt problem
with it: The World Does Not Value Manual Information
Transfer! Organisations do not search for graduates who
know how to google, they search for those who are able to
process information and create new knowledge using
capabilities we learn only by thinking and reasoning in a
costly manner.
2

I have read course assignments for many years and it is
easy to see how extrinsically many students deal with
knowledge. The aim is simply to fill empty pages with text
and figures to fulfil some external requirements. The
OnePage method recognises the meaninglessness of such
information transfer and replaces it with a more
intrinsically rewarding method of creating one’s own ideas
and using one’s own reasoning and personality when
writing assignments. The intrinsic reward comes from the
nature of the mind itself being curious and looking for
meaningful things in life.
By limiting the treatment of any topic to one page makes
it possible for a student to really process things in their
own mind as they are not required to fill several pages with
copied material. The page limit makes it useless to transfer
material directly from internet as the space is needed for
creating meaningful personal treatment.
Students report both joy and hardship. Joy arises
whenever we are experiencing something authentically
true to us. We all know what learning feels like when our
thinking is genuinely affected and our perception of reality
changes. What makes the method difficult is the fact that
authentic learning takes energy and effort, and also skills
that we are not necessarily so familiar with.
The OnePage method aims at training particular type of
skills that are rare and known to be valuable throughout
our history. Those skills operate at the meta level, meaning
they are valuable for us in all instances, throughout the
world, and for the entire duration of our lives. The skills are
cognitive, meaning they are connected to our ways of
thinking, and they produce something called critical
thinking, for which the world is willing to reward you
abundantly. If you understand what the above sentence
3

indicates, I need not explain the logic of reality in any
greater detail. What I do need to explain, however, is what
critical thinking is and how it operates, and especially how
it can be trained by this method.
4

Critical thinking
The concept of critical thinking may bring many kinds of
sensations; some may consider critical thinking as
something to be avoided simply because criticism feels
unpleasant due to its common usage connected with fault
finding and critical judgement. We don’t generally like to be
criticised.
Critical thinking is however, when properly perceived,
central to the very sense of being human because it
facilitates knowledge about us and our environment.
Paradoxically, the modern person can live quite easily
without connection to critical thinking because we are
utilising knowledge that has been created by critical
thinking of others before us.
The urgency of critical thinking becomes highlighted in
our time of increasing flow of (dis)information which
decreases our ability to make sense of the world. People
have increasing difficulties in distinguishing between real
and unreal, between causes and effects, etc. So, the task at
hand can be argued to be both vital and urgent.
There are many ways we can categorise elements of
critical thinking. I will provide here a simple taxonomy that
directs our attention toward the operation of the mind.
There is plenty of literature on the subject, so for those of
you who want to dive into the world of critical thinking, a
whole tradition is to be found going through the Greek
philosophers (500 BCE
) in the West as well as the
Buddhist thinkers (also 500 BCE) in the East.
 BCE = Before the Common Era.
5

Critical thinking is connected to us in two ways: as 1) an
inherent inclination or trait we are born with and as 2) a
skill that can be practised and cultivated. All of us benefit
from the practice as the results are life-changing and there
is no limit to be found beyond which your mind could not
penetrate. Critical thinking contains 4 elements that we
need to focus and practise here:
❖ Flexibility of thinking and open-mindedness
❖ Abandoning our earlier opinions, beliefs and
(pre)conceptions
❖ Creative thinking
❖ Reflexivity
Flexibility refers to acceptance of new information as
candidate for knowledge. We need to be both open and
alert at the same time which makes our practice both
interesting and demanding. Accepting new information
without scrutiny would be uncritical thinking and rejection
without proper inspection would manifest rigidity of
thinking. So, we need to administer a proper balance
between them, and that takes practice. Readiness to
abandon false (pre)conceptions and ideas is also important.
We need to practise to learn when and with what type of
evidence ideas should be dismissed. Most importantly, we
need to find causes for both accepting and dismissing ideas
and conceptions; and these causes need to be credible and
valid foremost to ourselves and secondarily to others.
Creative thinking enters the picture when we examine
information. Newly adopted information requires
interpretation; we need to fit the new elements into the
existing structure of our knowledge. Both the newly
adopted information and our knowledge structure are
adaptable to some extent. How and to what extent each
adapts to the other depends on the elasticity of our minds.
6

Creativity helps us to spontaneously connect between
conceptions and create further ideas. And as soon as this is
said, in marches the reflexive guard who needs to
investigate that we are not doing anything entirely silly.
Reflexivity refers to a thought process by which we make
our thinking intelligible to ourselves. Without reflexivity we
would never learn how we come to learn things and how
we come to think of things we do. Without it we would
remain victims in our own lives, disconnected from the
decision-maker who we should become.
It is entirely possible to live through our lives without a
clear understanding of how our main cognitive capacity
operates in the world of ideas, feelings, opinions,
(pre)conceptions and knowledge. Critical thinking is a twoway arrow; one end points outside and the other inside of
us. By practising critical thinking we learn not only about
how the world operates but more importantly how we
ourselves operate in the world. Through diligent practice
self-knowledge arises and mastery in it liberates us. That
should be something worthwhile (beside the tiny sideeffect of the world rewarding anyone with this capability).
7

Assignments
In this section my purpose is to explain how to write
course assignments utilising the OnePage critical thinking
methodology. First, work on the assignment needs to be
divided into two distinguishable and separate phases:
(1) Research phase
(2) Reporting phase
These two phases need to be kept separate because they
serve different purposes and problems arise whenever
writers mix these phases: a hasty writer may try to
construct her page while still investigating, when things
appear anything but clear to her. She ends up constructing
the page by the use of reasoning provided by others (in the
internet and elsewhere).
The research phase begins by studying the theories and
models given in the assignment. These theories and models
serve a purpose and the aim of the student is to understand
how they operate and how they are related to other things.
The research phase requires extensive use of internet
and databases. When going through articles and texts one is
constantly bombarded by ideas and arguments provided by
their authors. How is one to know what to believe and what
to reject?
The less we initially know about something, the more
disordered new inputs of information may appear at the
beginning. By going through a number of different sources,
recurrent patterns start to appear, however, and order
begins to take shape. But this does not happen
automatically, or without effort. Consider two different
types of attitudes toward things we come across and which
8

are new to us. First one can be called “short-term costminimisation” because anyone following this principle will
systematically discard anything they are not already
familiar with. They do so because it saves costs in the shortterm as investigation necessarily involves effort and use of
energy. The second type of attitude can be called “long-term
improvement” because this person does not see the shortterm cost as a cost at all, but instead, as an investment. Her
inquisitive mind collects constantly new inputs and her
categorising capacity rearranges phenomena into new
orders. In time these constantly reshaping orders correlate
with reality to a degree unattainable by any costminimising principle.
The research phase comes to a close when a plan, a
strategy starts to take shape. The writer understands now
how the concepts and models of the assignment operate
and how they can be applied in a real world setting, and
how they can help us make sense of phenomena inside and
outside of organisations.
The research phase may have accumulated much
material and now the writer has to carefully choose what to
include in the report. A common tendency needs to be
resisted where everything one has collected is poured into
the report for the reader to pick and choose. The page
limitation is there to drive the writer to critically think and
select when constructing the page. One cannot say
everything there is, so one needs to have a meaningful plan
to what one wants to say.
There is a general pattern to be followed when applying
critical thinking to economic phenomena. Our task is
typically to apply a given theory or model in an empirical
setting and analyse its intended and unintended
consequences. An analysis consists of examination of causal
9

relations between phenomena. The writer’s task is then to
show how different things lead to other things and thus
how the model operates in reality. The reader is interested
in seeing how the mind of the writer perceives and
differentiates things, how it reasons and concludes, what
type of things it takes for granted and how it provides
evidence. Much too seldom a train of thought can be found
going something like this: “This happens for the following
reason …”.
When searching for relevant information for
assignments, we need to use sources (often in digital form)
extensively. The OnePage method does not do away the
need to filter information, it only advises us not to present
discovered information as an outcome of our own learning.
However, we need to google extensively in order to create a
solid understanding of the arrays of applications of models
and theories. However, googling alone does not always
bring information of expected quality, so we may need to
use databases that contain edited articles and journals as
well. To what extent this is so depends on the subject at
hand.
Since we need to use referential information when
constructing our assignments, referencing standards come
into play. And since our OnePage method limits the space
available, we don’t want to waste that limited space by
listing references at the end of each assignment, that is, at
the end of each page. Instead, we attach a separate list of
references at the end of the course report containing
references of all assignments.
Listing references in a standard way at the end of the
course report should be unproblematic. How about the
other end of a reference, the one in the text, inside brackets,
like (Kaisla 2012), what should we do about those? This
10

question may sound silly to those who know what
referencing is all about, but around 50 % of students do not
make any in-text references. They simply create a list of
references at the end of the report and consider the job
done. The in-text reference is of course what the whole
business of referencing is about. By the exact placing and
style of that reference marking we show the reader how we
are using the source information.
Contrary to the reporting guidelines of the school, the
overall layout of the course report can be designed to stand
out in the crowd and attract the reader. This is because
visual design can be an important sales factor and training
in that competency is also beneficial. Another central
guideline deals with the tiny but important detail of row
height. Use only row height of 1 and not the 1.5 as
instructed in the reporting guidelines of the school. The
rationale for this should be self-evident as our space is
limited to one page only (and besides the 1.5 row height is
a residual from the stone age when space was needed
between the lines in hard copies).
11

Lectures and discussions
Our courses consist typically of lectures or discussions
and assignments that are pursued in groups or individually.
The spirit of lectures varies greatly, depending on various
elements, such as skills and moods and motivations of the
students. To enhance our mutual understanding of the
purpose of lectures, let us look into their nature a bit.
Lectures are essentially about thinking together. This
means we get together to think and exchange our
understandings and views on themes by the method of
reasoning. Critical thinking can on the one hand be active
exchange of views and arguments among the participants,
yet on the other hand, it can be silent reflection while the
teacher opens new themes and horizons. The thing that
connects all variations of critical thinking is our
concentration on a mutual theme which is moving and
developing during the discourse.
What I said about the nature of lectures above looks
fairly easy and straight forward to understand. Yet in
reality pursuing such a simple goal can sometimes be
extremely difficult. Concentrating on a theme and listening
to what other people have to say can become tiresome,
especially if one’s mind is not engaged. Degreasing span of
concentration is a universal topic in education. A popular
remedy is to bring entertainment, replicating tools and
methods students already enjoy using such as games and
gadgets.
Critical thinking operates contrary to entertainment
factors, however, because if it did not, everyone would be a
millionaire. Critical thinking becomes developed by critical
thinking, and it is precisely with this skill that students are
12

at their weakest. If you have never experienced
independence in thinking, accepting your self as the primary
source of the thinking activity, it is very hard to perceive
how it works. I cannot blame the students for not having
been offered something that should have been throughout
their schooling: The European Union has in 2006 put
critical thinking into the centre of life-long learning
capabilities. Yet, instead of promoting independence of
thinking, universities educate politically correct followers.
How do I know this? I have met countless students in and
from universities around the world, and if they collectively
lack something, it is independent and reliable thinking.
What could explain this systematic deficiency in the very
core of capabilities that we should master? The answer lies
in how conventions operate in this world. See, many
teachers themselves have no experience of critical thinking
because they have gone through the same domestication
that they now offer to their students; that learning is about
collecting and memorising facts about reality as presented
in books and elsewhere.
Sometimes students may be confused about the task of
the teacher in this era of information overflow. They may
wonder why the teacher doesn’t provide them with a
ready-made path to “the correct solution”. The task of a
teacher becomes clearer when we understand how this
thinking together operates. There needs to be knowledge
present to give guidance to the moving themes, but what is
essential in learning is the personal processing of thoughts
interconnected with the shared ideas of the group. Thus
solutions may vary, but the central point is how we arrive
at our own conclusions, that is, how we construct our very
own path of reasoning, starting from an open question and
ending somewhere we can, looking back, clearly see the
13

realistic explanations and justifications for our reasoning.
We are inescapably and mutually interdependent but the
only one who can make this world understandable to us is
our own mind.

To meet the requirements set by your teacher for creating document assignments, here are some tailored Word settings and considerations that align with the OnePage method that emphasizes critical thinking and minimizes direct information transfer from the internet:

1. **Page Setup**:
   - Set your document to have a 1-page limit to encourage concise and thoughtful writing.

2. **Margins and Layout**:
   - Standardize margins (typically 1 inch all around) to maximize space.
   - Choose a readable font and size (Times New Roman 12pt is common in academia) to maintain clarity.

3. **Paragraph Settings**:
   - Use single line spacing (as per your teacher's instruction) instead of 1.5 because of the one-page constraint.
   - Adjust paragraph spacing to be minimal, ensuring more content fits on the page.

4. **Text Styles**:
   - Use headings and subheadings to organize your thoughts clearly.
   - Emphasize critical points, perhaps with bold or italics, to enhance readability.

5. **References**:
   - Utilize a consistent in-text citation style (APA, MLA, etc.) to reference ideas that originated from external sources.
   - Attach a separate reference list at the end of the document to avoid cluttering your one-page assignment.

6. **Proofreading Tools**:
   - Enable spelling and grammar checks to ensure your writing is polished and professional.

7. **Review and Feedback**:
   - If collaborating or seeking feedback, use 'Track Changes' and 'Comments' for revisions without altering the original text.

By incorporating these settings, you align your Word document with the expectations of critical thought and minimize the reliance on uncritical information transfer, following the OnePage method's philosophy. These settings will support producing work that is reflective of personal understanding and synthesis rather than merely presenting found information.

Through the eyes of public interest, Snowden’s breach of trust reveals itself not in black and white but in shades of gray demanding thorough consideration.
Howe (2019), in analyzing Snowden’s breach, brings attention to the use and abuse of cryptographic keys and digital certificates, the hidden essentials of digital security. Their critical assessment posits that Snowden’s ability to exfiltrate data might be rooted in his deep understanding of these systems, possibly abusing them to mask his activities from the eyes of the NSA (Howe, 2019). This opens an entirely new topic to the debate, underscoring the shakiness of trust in the cybersecurity domain.
Snowden pushes us to confront the rift between government actions for security and the public’s right to privacy. This act represents more than policy. It embodies a shifting consciousness towards the nature of our private lives.
As we digest Snowden’s case, there is an ongoing reformation of how democracy and surveillance intersect. The dialogue he sparked is less about the man and more about the world he revealed. A world where privacy concerns are tangled deeply with the threads of technological advancement and where the protection of one’s digital shadow has become pivotal in the broader discourse on freedom.

Assignment 5 – Internet of things and of behaviours
Exploring the realm where psychology meets data, the Internet of Behaviors is all about piecing together our digital traces to predict and sway our daily choices. Think of IoB as tech’s next step in understanding us better, using the breadcrumbs we leave via smart gadgets and online activity. This data isn’t just numbers; it’s a goldmine for sectors like healthcare, helping patients stick to their doctor’s orders, for stores to shape the shopping vibe, or for car insurers to nudge us towards that safe driver discount.
But with all this data-harvesting comes a real need to keep things locked down and private. Imagine a digital lock-and-key system that’s always getting stronger, laws that keep our data under wraps, and being in the know about keeping our online selves under our control. IoB demands we get savvy about how our info is shared and used.
And here’s the thing: it’s got to be on the up-and-up. Using IoB means playing fair—getting clear yeses from people before scooping up their data, making sure that the data gathered is only what’s really needed, and keeping everyone in the loop about what’s happening with their info. Setting up some ground rules industry-wide means that everyone’s working from the same playbook, making sure this powerful new tech stays trustworthy and doesn’t cross the line.
Talking about crossing lines, IoB systems need to chat securely with one another. It’s like having a secret handshake that keeps all the connected parts safe from prying eyes, guarding against any sneaky cyber tricks.
In a nutshell, IoB could be a game-changer in getting to know human behavior and enhancing our lives, provided it’s harnessed with care—respecting privacy, sticking to strong security routines, and always keeping it ethical. Just imagine the possibilities when all these pieces come together just right.
